note_en:
Two weeks ago I stumbled upon the concept of  prime portraits. In short, prime portraits are pictures in which each color is assigned a number. When we align every pixel, the result should be a prime number. Because I could not find the code used in the article, I decided to recreate these prime portraits in Python – in color! Below is the Jupyter Notebook with the code ( also available on Github) I used to generate the Mona Lisa:
   A prime portrait is a prime number formatted as a matrix with X digits per line. When we select a color for each digit, we can generate an image.
 Instead of doing this for many prime numbers and color schemes until you find something that resembles a known image, I have turned the process around. I have taken iconic images, such as the Mona Lisa and Starry Night, and converted them to images with only 10 colors. I assigned a digit to each color. Then I generated many similar images with a little bit of ‘noise’ added. The noise changed the colors in the images slightly, and thus the digits. If the digits in the image formed a prime number, I found a prime portrait!
 import  matplotlib.pyplot  as  plt % matplotlib  inline import  numpy  as  np import  skimage.transform import  skimage.io from  sklearn.cluster  import  KMeans import  scipy.misc from  PIL  import  Image ,  ImageFont ,  ImageDraw import  numpy  as  np from  multiprocessing  import  Pool import  time import  random import  warnings
 When working with extremely long primes, the naive method of testing each divisor will take way too much time. There are a lot of prime numbers and checking your number for each possible divisor takes too long to test a lot of prime portraits.
 Instead we take a probabilistic approach to the prime numbers. The  Miller Rabin test uses heuristics to determine if a number is (probably) prime or not. I downloaded the  following code for the Miller-Rabin primality test from Rosetta code to check for primality:
 def  try_composite ( a ,  d ,  n ,  s ):  # test the base a to see whether it is a witness for the compositeness of n  if  pow ( a ,  d ,  n )  ==  1 :  return  False  for  i  in  range ( s ):  if  pow ( a ,  2 ** i  *  d ,  n )  ==  n - 1 :  return  False  return  True  # n is definitely composite  def  is_probable_prime ( n ,  mrpt_num_trials  =  5 ):  """  Miller-Rabin primality test.   A return value of False means n is certainly not prime. A return value of  True means n is very likely a prime.  """  assert  n  >=  2  # special case 2  if  n  ==  2 :  return  True  # ensure n is odd  if  n  %  2  ==  0 :  return  False  # write n-1 as 2**s * d  # repeatedly try to divide n-1 by 2  s  =  0  d  =  n - 1  while  True :  quotient ,  remainder  =  divmod ( d ,  2 )  if  remainder  ==  1 :  break  s  +=  1  d  =  quotient  assert ( 2 ** s  *  d  ==  n - 1 )   for  i  in  range ( mrpt_num_trials ):  a  =  random . randrange ( 2 ,  n )  if  try_composite ( a ,  d ,  n ,  s ):  return  False   return  True  # no base tested showed n as composite assert  is_probable_prime ( 2 ) assert  is_probable_prime ( 3 ) assert  not  is_probable_prime ( 4 ) assert  is_probable_prime ( 5 ) assert  not  is_probable_prime ( 123456789 ) primes_under_1000  =  [ i  for  i  in  range ( 2 ,  1000 )  if  is_probable_prime ( i )] assert  len ( primes_under_1000 )  ==  168 assert  primes_under_1000 [ - 10 :]  ==  [ 937 ,  941 ,  947 ,  953 ,  967 ,  971 ,  977 ,  983 ,  991 ,  997 ] assert  is_probable_prime ( 6438080068035544392301298549614926991513861075340134\ 3291807343952413826484237063006136971539473913409092293733259038472039\ 7133335969549256322620979036686633213903952966175107096769180017646161\ 851573147596390153 ) assert  not  is_probable_prime ( 7438080068035544392301298549614926991513861075340134\ 3291807343952413826484237063006136971539473913409092293733259038472039\ 7133335969549256322620979036686633213903952966175107096769180017646161\ 851573147596390153 )
 The basis of the algorithm is generating images with only 10 possible color values. I did not know what a common approach to this is, so I thought applying a  k-means algorithm would work. To do this, I reshaped an input image (so each pixel is a point in 3D space), clustered this, and used the 10 created clusters as color values. Sidenote: when your hammer is machine learning, everything looks like a nail…
 Important for the prime generation is that you add a little bit of noise to the original image to create images that look the same, but are slightly different. This means that you created a different number, which is a new chance for us to find a prime number.
 def  get_k_means ( image ):  pointcloud  =  np . reshape ( image ,  ( - 1 ,  3 ))  kmeans  =  KMeans ( n_clusters = 10 ) . fit ( pointcloud )  return  kmeans def  create_numbered_image ( image ,  kmeans ):  """  Turns an RGB image into an image with the labels of clusters of the supplied kmeans classifier.   """  # apply noise  stdev  =  np . std ( image )  random_noise  =  np . random . random_sample ( size = image . shape ) * ( stdev / 3 )  image  =  image  +  random_noise   orig_shape  =  image . shape  image  =  np . reshape ( image ,  ( - 1 ,  3 ))  numbered_image  =  kmeans . predict ( image )  numbered_image  =  np . reshape ( numbered_image ,  orig_shape [: 2 ])   # make sure the end is uneven  if  numbered_image [ - 1 , - 1 ] % 2 == 0 :  numbered_image [ - 1 , - 1 ]  +=  1  return  numbered_image def  numbered_image_to_normal_image ( numbered_image ,  kmeans ):  """  Turns an image with only values between 0 and 9 into a colored image by using the cluster centers   of the supplied kmeans classifier.   """  shape  =  ( numbered_image . shape [ 0 ],  numbered_image . shape [ 1 ],  3 )  image  =  np . zeros ( shape )  for  label ,  color  in  zip ( range ( 10 ),  kmeans . cluster_centers_ ):  image [ numbered_image == label ]  =  color  return  image
 Here I create a few examples of small numerised images of the Mona Lisa. If you look closely you can see each image is slightly different, but you can still recognise the original picture. This is crucial for us, as this allows us to generate multiple numerised images that we can test for primality.
 def  load_and_resize_image ( filename ,  resize_factor = 18 ):  image  =  skimage . io . imread ( filename )  image  =  image . astype ( np . float64 )  image  =  image  /  255.   oldshape  =  image . shape  with  warnings . catch_warnings ():  warnings . simplefilter ( "ignore" )  resized_image  =  skimage . transform . resize ( image ,  ( oldshape [ 0 ] // resize_factor ,  oldshape [ 1 ] // resize_factor ))  return  resized_image resized_image  =  load_and_resize_image ( 'input_pictures/monalisa.jpg' ) kmeans  =  get_k_means ( resized_image ) N_IMAGES  =  3 f ,  axarr  =  plt . subplots ( 1 , N_IMAGES ) for  index  in  range ( N_IMAGES ):  n_image  =  create_numbered_image ( resized_image ,  kmeans )  normal_image  =  numbered_image_to_normal_image ( n_image ,  kmeans )  axarr [ index ] . imshow ( normal_image )
 I had to create some helper functions to return strings, integers, and load and show images.
 def  image_to_number ( numbered_image ):  to_be_number  =  numbered_image . reshape ( - 1 )  as_string  =  '' . join ([ str ( int ( x ))  for  x  in  to_be_number ])  as_int  =  int ( as_string )  return  as_int ,  as_string def  show_and_save_image ( image ,  n_image ,  filename ,  fontsize = 16 ):  oldshape  =  image . shape  resized_image =  scipy . misc . imresize ( image ,( oldshape [ 0 ] * fontsize ,  oldshape [ 1 ] * fontsize ),  interp = 'nearest' )  img  =  Image . fromarray ( resized_image ) . convert ( "RGBA" )  txt  =  Image . new ( 'RGBA' ,  img . size ,  ( 255 , 255 , 255 , 0 ))  draw  =  ImageDraw . Draw ( txt )  font  =  ImageFont . truetype ( "pirulen rg.ttf" ,  fontsize )  for  y_i ,  totype  in  enumerate ( n_image ):  for  x_i ,  letter  in  enumerate ( totype ):  xpos  =  x_i  *  fontsize  +  1  ypos  =  y_i  *  fontsize  if  letter  ==  1 :  xpos  +=  4  draw . text (( xpos ,  ypos ), str ( letter ),( 255 , 255 , 255 ,  128 ), font = font )  img  =  Image . alpha_composite ( img ,  txt )   img . save ( filename )  plt . figure ( figsize = ( 20 , 20 ))  plt . imshow ( img )  plt . show () def  result_filename ( filename ):  return  filename . split ( '.' )[ 0 ]  +  "-prime.png"
 To verify if something is a good prime portrait I made the following helper functions:
 def  is_good_prime_portrait ( n_image ):  integer ,  string  =  image_to_number ( n_image )  if  is_probable_prime ( integer ):   return  integer ,  string ,  n_image  else :  return  None   def  print_result ( string ,  n_image ):  print ( string )  print ( "-" * 100 )  for  line  in  n_image :  print ( '' . join ([ str ( x )  for  x  in  line ]))
 At first I created a single-threaded solution, which worked. However, my laptop has multiple cores and could work on multiple numbers at the same time, so I rewrote my initial approach in a multi-threaded fashion:
 def  print_result ( string ,  n_image ):  print ( "Found a result: "  +  "-" * 100 )  print ( string )  print ( "Represented as portrait:"  +  "-" * 100 )  for  line  in  n_image :  print ( '' . join ([ str ( x )  for  x  in  line ])) def  multi_threaded_prime_generator ( resized_image ,  kmeans ,  filename ,  threads = 4 ,  log_process = True ):  image_generator  =  ( create_numbered_image ( resized_image ,  kmeans )  for  _  in  range ( 1000000 ))  start  =  time . time ()   with  Pool ( threads )  as  pool :  results  =  pool . imap_unordered ( is_good_prime_portrait ,  image_generator )  total_results  =  0   for  result  in  results :  total_results  +=  1   # Possibly log time spend searching this prime number  if  log_process  and  total_results % 30 == 0 :  elapsed  =  time . time ()  elapsed  =  elapsed  -  start  print ( "Seconds spent in (function name) is  {}  time per result:  {} " . format ( str ( elapsed ),  str ( elapsed / total_results )))   if  result  !=  None :   # Found a prime number, print it and save it!  integer ,  string ,  n_image  =  result  print_result ( string ,  n_image )  normal_image  =  numbered_image_to_normal_image ( n_image ,  kmeans )  plt . imshow ( normal_image )  plt . show ()  show_and_save_image ( normal_image ,  n_image ,  result_filename ( filename ))  break   def  search_prime_portrait ( filename ,  resize_factor = 16 ,  log_process = True ,  threads = 4 ):  resized_image  =  load_and_resize_image ( filename ,  resize_factor = resize_factor )  print ( "Working with size "  +  str ( resized_image . shape ))  kmeans  =  get_k_means ( resized_image )  multi_threaded_prime_generator ( resized_image ,  kmeans ,  filename ,  log_process = log_process ,  threads = threads )
 Now that we defined all functionality, it’s finally time to generate the prime portraits! Play around with it and try to feed your own images. Note that you can pass the resize_factor variable to the function ‘search prime portrait’. The bigger the resize_factor, the faster you can find a prime portrait. See below for more information on the exponential time it takes to generate larger prime portraits.
 folder  =  'input_pictures/' filenames  =  [  folder  +  'monalisa.jpg' ,  #folder + 'sunflowers.jpg',  #folder + 'starrynight.jpg' ] filename  =  filenames [ 0 ] for  filename  in  filenames :  search_prime_portrait ( filename ,  resize_factor = 20 ,  log_process = False ,  threads = 4 )
 Working with size (19, 13, 3)Found a result: ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------6666666666662666626266262222222331222222222395146222066685731062280001758180008000853810000006688771802033388995386733331599578182871883701148387881881131880888181181188883118144141011855514411181111757388411111333881144814118814444114111444444441Represented as portrait:----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------6666666666662666626266262222222331222222222395146222066685731062280001758180008000853810000006688771802033388995386733331599578182871883701148387881881131880888181181188883118144141011855514411181111757388411111333881144814118814444114111444444441
 Apparently the  largest known prime number is 22338618 digits long. This would allow us to create a 4726 * 4726 prime portrait, which would be a prime portrait in 4K. I already enjoyed the big images of ‘Starry Night’, and I would absolutely love to have a 4K prime portrait of this painting.
 However: I noticed that finding prime numbers does not really scale. The length of the prime number grows quadratically with the resolution of your images, which causes big problems. Although the probability that you find a prime number only decreases linearly with the length of the prime number, it decreases quadratically when you get larger image resolutions. I plotted it in this image to show the effect up to 300×300:
  But what’s even worse is that computing time grows quadratically with the length of the prime number. I also plotted this for my laptop up to an image resolution of 30×30:
 def  time_expected_img ( w , h ):  order  =  w * h  start  =  time . time ()   for  _  in  range ( 100 ):  posprim  =  random . randrange ( 10 ** ( order - 1 ),  10 ** order )   a  =  is_probable_prime ( posprim )  end  =  time . time ()  return  end  -  start  test_range  =  range ( 2 , 30 , 4 ) time_needed  =  [ time_expected_img ( x , x )  for  x  in  test_range ] plt . plot ( test_range ,  time_needed ) plt . title ( "Seconds expected to calculate if an XxY image is a prime portrait " ) plt . xlabel ( "Width image" ) plt . ylabel ( "Expected seconds" )
 Combining both linear curves you can easily estimate the expected time you need to find a portrait of a certain size:
 total_needed  =  [ trials_expected_img ( x , x ) * time_expected_img ( x , x )  for  x  in  test_range ] plt . plot ( test_range ,  total_needed ) plt . title ( "Seconds expected to find an XxY prime portrait " ) plt . xlabel ( "Width image" ) plt . ylabel ( "Expected seconds" )
 If you can find a more efficient Python implementation of a probabilistic prime checker, or implement it in a language more suitable to high demand computation, you can lower the time you need to generate these portraits. Each additional computer core also reduces this time a bit, but for now we can not expect 4K prime portraits anytime soon.
 The last couple of weeks I really enjoyed letting my computer find prime portraits. Here are some of the artworks my computer found:   Sunflowers by Van Gogh   Great wave by Hokusai   Girl with a pearl earring by Vermeer
  Although pictures of existing art often turn our pretty well, it is quite difficult to create good looking prime portraits of people. There is often not enough contrast in the resulting images, which uses only ten colors. I decided to try some portraits for people I like to follow online. Images that turned our well are:
  Writes interesting articles in a Dutch newspaper every Saturday, and got featured a while ago in the Dutch comic called “Donald Duck”:
  Makes interesting videos about mathematical concepts on the Numberphile Youtube channel. Also: has a cute dog 😉
    As I put  the code online you can try to generate some prime portraits for yourself! If you really think I should try a certain artwork, feel free to reach out to me. Also: if you have a good idea what to do with these results, feel free to reach out. I know that I personally am going to hang the prime version of Starry Night on my wall!
title: 按素数绘画（2018）

note:
伊朗最高核科学家莫森·法赫里扎德（Mohsen Fakhrizadeh）被暗杀的场景的3D模型揭示了一个令人惊讶的结果：所有发射的子弹似乎都来自同一地方，显然支持了有关这场杀戮的备受争议的官方解释。但是，这种情况可能不止是遇到3D眼睛。
  伊朗当局坚持说，暗杀是在停在路边的日产皮卡车后部用遥控机关枪进行的，然后将其炸毁。其他说法表明，袭击是由多达12名枪手进行的，他们随后逃离现场。
  这种建模是由不断增长的在线OSINT（开放源代码情报）分析师队伍之一使用免费提供的数据来调查犯罪的方法，这种方法在Bellingcat网站上广为人知。这位匿名分析员使用视频播放器Putin Is A Virus，并得到OsintEditor社区的支持，是一位视频游戏设计师，他使用Unity 3D模拟软件重新创建了杀戮现场。
      他的模型是交互式的，带有解释性注释，可以在这里找到。 （需要键盘和鼠标）。
    这位分析师告诉我：“我参加了调查，认为我可以反驳我认为是自动炮塔的荒谬理论。” “第一步是精确绘制所有内容，包括弹孔。一旦将所有东西都放在最准确的位置，我就会为进出孔分配一个数字。”
    这给出了留给五个子弹的120个进出孔的可能组合。
  “然后针对120种组合中的每种子弹，我创建了10,000条可能的轨迹，假设最大水平偏差为2.5度，最大垂直偏差为6度，因为子弹一旦在车内就可能偏离。” 
除去那些不可能的子弹后，仍有超过200万种子弹弹道。然后，分析人员将场景划分为球形网格，称为体积单元或体素，每个直径为0.4米。如果一个球体与被分析的五个子弹的每一个相交有一个或多个可能的轨迹，则将其视为有效的射击位置。
  分析师总结说：“似乎有一个可能的射击位置负责所有弹孔，而该位置恰好在拾音器后部。” “最终我得到了与预期相反的结果。”
  该方法旨在查看单个点火位置是否可能，但不能排除多个点火位置的可能性（无论多么不可能）。
  伊朗当局声称，这辆汽车在散热器上被子弹击中后停了下来，那里有损坏的迹象，然后被开火。道路上的血迹表明，法赫扎迪（Fakhrizadeh）被带出汽车，在道路上死亡，或者他可能被第二次爆炸击中。
  据英国Matrix Legal＆amp; Company公司数十年经验的弹道专家Philip Boyce称，这些结果令人印象深刻。法医。
  “如果您有一个入口和一个出口孔，则软件可以高度自信地绘制轨迹，” Boyce说。
  博伊斯指出，像这样的远程攻击要比在几米远的射手情况下更容易对准弹孔。 
博伊斯说：“有多个弹孔，射手越远，过程越准确。” “在150米处，您应该能够找到它们。这是假设目标和射手是静止的。”
  使用的武器可能类似于SmartShooter今年早些时候推出的Smash Hopper便携式远程发射站。可以装配机枪或其他武器进行精确攻击；制造商声称它是高度准确的，适合伏击和远程进攻行动。在叙利亚和其他最近发生冲突的地方，已使用针对机器人伺服器的即兴遥控武器。
    根据C / O Futures，LLC的反恐专家Robert Bunker的说法，使用这种武器并不是完全不可行的。
  “伊朗人的叙述让人想起奥斯汀·鲍尔斯电影中的邪恶博士阴谋。尽管不太可能，但它并不像现在听起来那么牵强。”邦克说。 “过去我曾在自己的著作中讨论过一种威胁情景，即简易爆炸装置在使用恐怖分子部署的遥控狙击步枪后，便被用来摧毁它。”
  但是，邦克指出，我们所知道的一切都是由伊朗当局控制的，伊朗当局只发布了他们希望共享的信息，没有一个可以被核实。
  “问题的一部分是在事件现场已删除，添加或上演的内容。最好的错误信息是基于某些事实，并在读者填补空白的情况下产生了凝聚力的叙述。这样，我们的大脑就会对自己不利。”邦克说。
  邦克指出了有关官方帐户的众多问题。特别是，他被据说陪伴着法赫里扎德的保镖所困扰。为什么没有迹象表明他们在行动中发挥了作用？为什么预备警卫没有发现埋伏车？为什么Fakhrizadeh的车辆开火时会停下来？为什么伊朗人声称法赫扎扎德的车辆是防弹的，而事实并非如此？ （可以很合理地猜测，其中的大部分可能掩盖了安全细节上的不足，这些细节完全无法保护已知目标或逮捕凶手。） 
我们无法告诉我们经过精心设计的犯罪现场是实际现场还是完全由当局上演。
  博伊斯指出：“通过设置汽车并开枪射击，可能会伪造场景，这将产生完全相同的结果。”
  邦克说：“我们的顾问公司正在进行的新法证分析表明，其中涉及一个小型杀戮小组。” “伊朗新闻稿纯粹是对发生的实际事件的捏造。”
  ＆amp; Putin是一种病毒＆＃39;但是请注意，场景的图像是从事件发生几小时后开始的，几乎没有机会重新拍摄，可以通过随后拍摄的视频来确认其位置。伊朗人没有提出“远程炮塔”直到两天后再进行解释。
  此案远未解决，而且组合中肯定存在错误信息。杀人的方法，更不用说杀手的身份了，还远不能确定。但是，配备有越来越复杂的软件工具的开源情报社区的技能和独创性可能会使其打开并证明发生了什么方式。

note_en:
A 3D model of the scene of the assassination of Iran’s top nuclear scientist, Mohsen Fakhrizadeh, reveals a surprising result: all the bullets fired seem to have originated from the same place, apparently supporting the  highly contested official explanation of the killing. But there may be more to the case than meets the 3D eye.
  The Iranian authorities have insisted that the assassination was carried out with  a remote-controlled machinegun in the back of a Nissan pickup truck parked by the side of the road, which then blew up. Other accounts suggest the attack was carried out by up to 12 gunmen who then fled the scene.
  The modelling was carried by one of a growing army of online OSINT (Open-Source Intelligence) analysts using freely-available data to investigate crimes, an approach made famous by the  Bellingcat website. The anonymous analyst, who uses the handle  Putin Is A Virus, and was supported by the  OsintEditor community, is a video game designer and used the Unity 3D simulation software to recreate the scene of the killing.
      His model, which is interactive and comes with explanatory notes,  can be found here. (Keyboard and mouse required).
    “I approached the investigation believing I could disprove what I considered the absurd theory of an automated turret,” the analyst told me. “The first step was to precisely map everything, bullet holes included. Once I had everything in the most accurate place possible I assigned a number to entry and exit holes.”
    This gave 120 possible combinations of entry and exit holes for the five bullets which left them.
  “For each bullet of each of the 120 combinations, I then created 10,000 possible trajectories assuming a max horizontal deviation of 2.5 degrees and max vertical deviation of 6 degrees, as bullets could have deviated once inside the vehicle.”
    After removing those that were impossible, there were still over two million possible bullet trajectories. The analyst then divided the scene into a grid of spheres, known as volume cells or voxels, each 0.4 meters in diameter. If a sphere intersected with one or more possible trajectories for each of the five bullets analyzed, it would be considered a valid firing position.
  “It appears that there is a single, possible, firing position responsible for all of the bullet holes, and that position is exactly in the pickup rear,” concludes the analyst. “I ended up with the opposite result to what I expected.”
  The method was intended to see if a single firing position was possible, but cannot exclude the possibility, however improbable, of multiple firing positions.
  The Iranian authorities claim that the car stopped after it was hit by a bullet in the radiator — there are signs of damage there — and was then fired on. Bloodstains by the road suggest that Fakhrizadeh was carried out of the car and died on the road, or he may have been hit by a second burst.
  These results are impressive, according to Philip Boyce, a ballistics expert with decades of experience at U.K. company  Matrix Legal & Forensic.
  “If you have an entry and an exit hole, then software can plot the trajectory with a high degree of confidence,” says Boyce.
  Boyce notes that lining up bullet holes is more straightforward for long-range attacks like this one, rather than situations where the shooter is a few meters away.
  “With multiple bullet holes the process is more accurate the further away the shooter is,” says Boyce. “At 150 meters you should be able to pinpoint them. This is assuming that the target and the shooter are stationary.”
  The weapon used may be something like the  Smash Hopper portable remote firing station unveiled by SmartShooter earlier this year. This can be fitted with a machinegun or other weapon for precision attacks; the makers claim it is highly accurate and suitable for ambushes and remote offensive action.  Improvised remote-controlled weapons aimed with robot servos have been used in Syria and elsewhere in recent conflicts.
    The use of this type of weapon is not wholly implausible, according to counterterrorism expert Robert Bunker  of C/O Futures, LLC .
  “The Iranian account is reminiscent of a Dr. Evil plot from an Austin Powers movie. While improbable, it is not as far-fetched as it may currently sound,” says Bunker. “In my own writings in the past I have discussed a threat scenario in which an IED was used to destroy a terrorist deployed remote-controlled sniper rifle after it was utilized.”
  However, Bunker notes that everything we know is controlled by the Iranian authorities who have only released the information they wish to share, none of which can be verified.
  “Part of the issue is what has been removed, added or staged at the incident scene. The best misinformation is based on some truth and creates cohesive narratives with the reader filling in the blanks. This way our brains work against themselves,” says Bunker.
  Bunker points to numerous questions that surround the official account. In particular he is bothered by the carloads of bodyguards who supposedly accompanied Fakhrizadeh. Why is there no sign they played any part in the action? Why did the advance guards not spot the ambush vehicle? Why did Fakhrizadeh’s vehicle stop when fired on? And why do the Iranians claim that Fakhrizadeh’s vehicle was bulletproof when this is clearly not the case? (It's a fair guess that much of this may be covering up the incompetence of the security detail, which completely failed to protect a known target or apprehend the killers.)
  We have no way of telling whether the crime scene that has been so carefully modeled is the actual site, or wholly staged by the authorities.
  “The scene could be faked by setting the cars up and firing the shots, which would produce exactly the same results,” Boyce points out.
  “New forensic analysis our consultancy is conducting now suggests a small kill team was involved,” says Bunker. “Iranian news releases are pure fabrication of the actual events that occurred.”
  'Putin is a Virus' notes however that the images of the scene date from a few hours after the event, giving little chance to restage the shooting, whose location can be confirmed by videos taken soon afterwards. And the Iranians did not come up with the 'remote turret' explanation until two days afterwards.
  The case is far from solved, and there is certainly misinformation in the mix. The method of the killing, let alone the identity of the killers, is far from certain. But the skill and ingenuity of the open-source intelligence community equipped with increasingly sophisticated software tools may crack it open and prove what happened one way or another.
 Follow me on  Twitter.  Check out my  website or some of my other work  here.
title: 3D建模发现杀死伊朗科学家的令人惊讶的镜头来源
title_en: 3D Modeling Finds Surprising Source of the Shots That Killed Iranian Scientist

note:
国会在周一通过Covid救济和总额为2.3万亿美元的预算法案之后，民主党就已经要求更多。乔·拜登（Joe Biden）称这仅仅是“预付定金”，而议长南希·佩洛西（Nancy Pelosi）说，这是“第一步”。他们特别希望对各州进行救助，即使最新的人口普查报告显示各州的表现要好于他们声称的那样。
  州在最新的Covid法案中也做得很好。首先是耗资260亿美元的运输机构，机场和可铲铲的公共工程。这将有助于纽约的过境和港口当局减轻财务负担。
  尽管保险公司和联邦政府负担了测试费用，但各州也有220亿美元用于Covid测试和联系追踪。各国将使用它进行更多的预算回填。 《洛杉矶时报》上个月报道说，有25名洛杉矶县的消防员通过运送测试物资，平均加班时薪超过100,000美元，其中大部分由联邦《关怀法》基金补偿。
  教育将获得高达820亿美元的资金，其中约540亿美元将用于K-12学校，尽管许多学校关闭并且雇用了更少的员工。这大约是正常年份联邦政府在K-12上花费的金额。该法案还为低收入家庭提供了32亿美元的宽带资金，因此公立学校无需为此付费。
  州还将从联邦提高的每周300美元的失业救济金中受益，这在大多数州都是应税收入。失业救济甜味剂将特别帮助失业率更高的州，例如新泽西州（10.2％），夏威夷（10.1％），纽约（8.4％），康涅狄格州（8.2％）和加利福尼亚州（8.2％）。
note_en:
No sooner had Congress passed Covid relief and a budget bill totaling $2.3 trillion on Monday than Democrats were already demanding more. Joe Biden called it a mere “down payment” and Speaker Nancy Pelosi said it is “a first step.” They want bailouts for the states in particular, even as the latest Census report shows states are doing far better than they claim.
  States are also doing very well in this latest Covid bill. Start with the $26 billion for transit agencies, airports and shovel-ready public works. This will help New York’s transit and port authorities reduce their financial holes.
  There’s also $22 billion for the states for Covid testing and contact tracing, though insurers and the feds cover the cost of tests. States will use it for more budget backfill. The Los Angeles Times reported last month that 25 LA County firefighters had made more than $100,000 on average in overtime by ferrying test supplies, most of which was reimbursed by federal Cares Act funds.
  Education will get a whopping $82 billion, about $54 billion of which will go to K-12 schools though many are closed and employ fewer staff. That’s about as much as the federal government spends on K-12 in a normal year. The bill also provides $3.2 billion for broadband for low-income families, so public schools don’t have to pay for that.
  States will also benefit from the federal enhanced $300 weekly unemployment benefits, which are taxable income in most states. The jobless benefit sweetener will especially help states with higher unemployment such as New Jersey (10.2%), Hawaii (10.1%), New York (8.4%), Connecticut (8.2%) and California (8.2%).
title: 国家为贫困辩护，但他们充裕的现金
title_en: States plead poverty, but they are flush with cash
